examples.en: The flower is red.
There are many flowers in the garden.

entries.notes: Shared with Tagalog. Refers to any flowering bloom. Confidence: very high.

examples.en: The tree is tall.

examples.kp: Matas ya ing puno king bukid.
examples.en: The tree in the field is tall.

entries.notes: Shared with Tagalog. Refers to any tree. Confidence: very high.

examples.kp: Maragul ya ing babi king bukid.
examples.en: The pig in the field is big.

examples.kp: Munta la king bukid para magobra.
examples.en: They are going to the farm to work.

entries.notes: Shared with Tagalog. Refers to a farm, field, or rural land. Confidence: very high.

examples.kp: Maliwanag ya ing batuin king banua.
examples.en: The stars are bright in the sky.
The sky is bright today.

entries.notes: Kapampangan-native term for the physical sky, atmosphere, and weather. Distinct from 'langit' (heaven/paradise above). Confidence: high.

examples.en: The heavens are bright.
The heavens are bright today.

entries.notes: 'Langit' refers to heaven or the sky as a divine or celestial concept. For the physical weather-sky, Kapampangan uses 'banua'. Confidence: high.

examples.en: Let's go to church on Sunday.

examples.kp: Maragul ya ing simbahan king plasa.
examples.en: The church at the plaza is big.

entries.notes: Shared with Tagalog; from Spanish 'sembrar' root via Tagalog. Refers to a Christian church building. Confidence: very high.

examples.kp: Munta ku king tiangge king aldo.
examples.en: I am going to the market today.

examples.kp: Maragul ya ing tiangge king plasa.
examples.en: The market at the plaza is big.

entries.notes: Common Kapampangan and Tagalog word for a market stall or bazaar. The Tagalog term 'palengke' is also understood. Confidence: very high.

examples.kp: Libu la reng tao king plasa.
examples.en: Many people are at the plaza.

examples.kp: Atyu la reng tao king plasa.
examples.en: The people are at the plaza.

entries.notes: From Spanish 'plaza'. The central public square of a Philippine town or city. Confidence: very high; attested in everyday pack sentences.

examples.en: There are many jasmine flowers in the garden.

examples.kp: Malagu ya ing hardin king bale mi.
examples.en: The garden at our house is beautiful.

entries.notes: From Spanish 'jardín'. Refers to a cultivated garden, whether flowers or vegetables. Confidence: very high.

examples.kp: Maragul ya ing pista king plasa.
examples.en: The festival at the plaza is big.

examples.kp: Masaya la reng tao nung pista.
examples.en: People are happy during the festival.

entries.notes: From Spanish 'fiesta'. Refers to a town fiesta or religious feast day — a central event in Kapampangan community life. Confidence: very high.

examples.en: Put on your shoes.
He went inside the house.

entries.notes: Used both for entering a place ('sulud ya king bale' — he entered the house) and for putting something inside or on ('sulud me ing sapatus mu' — put on your shoes). Confidence: high.

examples.en: I have no money.
There is nobody at home.

entries.notes: 'Wala' negates existence — 'Wala ku pera' (I have no money). Contrast with 'ali'/'e' which negate verbs and adjectives. Shared with Tagalog. Confidence: very high.

examples.en: He/she is at the street corner.
Go to the street corner.

entries.notes: Spanish loanword from 'canto' (corner). Used widely in Filipino languages including Kapampangan. Refers to a street corner or intersection point. In everyday Kapampangan speech, people say 'king kanto' (at the corner) as a casual meeting place reference.

examples.en: We are on the coastal plain.

examples.kp: Maragul ya ing dayat ning Kapampangan.
examples.en: The coastal plain of Kapampangan is vast.

entries.notes: Refers to the flat coastal and delta region of Pampanga. Pampanga is historically a lowland province with a broad coastal plain. 'Dayat' describes this kind of flat, low-lying coastal geography. Equivalent to Tagalog 'kapatagan' (plain) or 'baybayin' (coast).

examples.kp: Malagu ya ing labuad ning bale mi.
examples.en: The surroundings of our house are beautiful.
There are many trees in the surrounding area.

entries.notes: Refers to the area surrounding a place — the neighborhood, vicinity, or environs. Equivalent to Tagalog 'paligid' (around/surrounding) or 'kapaligiran' (environment/surroundings). Commonly used when describing what is around a house, town, or landmark. Confidence: high; attested in Kapampangan vocabulary sources.

lessons.title: Outdoors & Nature: bulaklak, puno, bukid, banua, langit
lessons.intro: This lesson covers five words from the natural world. 'Bulaklak' (flower) and 'puno' (tree) are the most basic outdoor vocabulary. 'Bukid' (farm/field) is central to Kapampangan rural life — Pampanga is an agricultural heartland. 'Banua' and 'langit' both relate to the sky: 'banua' is the physical sky and weather overhead, while 'langit' carries the sense of heaven or the sky as a sacred space above.

exercises.prompt: Which word means "heaven" or the sky above?

accepted: the flower is red
the flower is red.
the tree is tall
the tree is tall.

exercises.prompt: Maragul ya ing babi king bukid.
accepted: the pig in the field is big
the pig in the field is big.
the pig at the farm is big
the pig at the farm is big.
the pig at the field is big
the pig at the field is big.

exercises.prompt: Maliwanag ya ing batuin king banua.
accepted: the stars are bright in the sky
the stars are bright in the sky.
the stars are bright in the atmosphere
the stars are bright in the atmosphere.

lessons.title: Community Places: simbahan, tiangge, plasa, hardin, pista
lessons.intro: These five words describe the places and events at the heart of Kapampangan community life. The 'simbahan' (church) anchors every town. The 'tiangge' (market) is where daily commerce happens. The 'plasa' (plaza) is the central gathering space facing the church. The 'hardin' (garden) brings beauty to home and public spaces. And the 'pista' (fiesta) is the beloved annual celebration — the biggest event in any Kapampangan town.

accepted: let's go to church on sunday
let's go to church on sunday.
let us go to church on sunday
let us go to church on sunday.

exercises.prompt: Libu la reng tao king plasa.
accepted: many people are at the plaza
many people are at the plaza.
many people are in the plaza
many people are in the plaza.
many people are at the town square
many people are at the town square.
many people are in the town square
many people are in the town square.
there are many jasmine flowers in the garden
there are many jasmine flowers in the garden.

lessons.title: Essential Words: sulud, wala
lessons.intro: This final lesson covers two high-utility words. 'Sulud' (to enter / to put in) is a flexible verb used both for going inside a place and for putting something on or in — including putting on shoes. 'Wala' (none / nothing / there is none) is the essential existential negation: use it to say something does not exist or you don't have something. Unlike 'ali' and 'e' which negate verbs and adjectives, 'wala' negates existence itself.

exercises.prompt: Which word means "to enter" or "to put in"?
Which word means "none" or "there is none"?

accepted: put on your shoes
put on your shoes.
he went inside the house
he went inside the house.
she went inside the house
she went inside the house.
he entered the house
he entered the house.
she entered the house
she entered the house.
i have no money
i have no money.
there is nobody at home
there is nobody at home.
there is no one at home
there is no one at home.

lessons.title: The Street Corner
lessons.intro: 'kanto' is the Kapampangan (and Filipino) word for street corner, borrowed from Spanish 'canto'. In everyday speech, 'king kanto' (at the corner) is a common meeting-place phrase.

accepted: he is at the street corner
she is at the street corner
he/she is at the street corner
he is at the corner
she is at the corner

lessons.title: The Coastal Plain: dayat
lessons.intro: 'dayat' refers to the flat coastal plain or lowland area — the kind of broad, flat landscape that defines much of Pampanga. 'Atyu kami king dayat' means we are on the coastal plain. It reflects the geographical identity of the Kapampangan homeland.

accepted: we are on the coastal plain
we are on the coastal plain.
we are in the lowland
we are in the lowland.
we are in the lowlands
we are in the lowlands.

lessons.title: Surroundings: labuad
lessons.intro: 'labuad' means the surroundings or vicinity of a place — the neighborhood or area around something. 'Malagu ya ing labuad ning bale mi' means the surroundings of our house are beautiful.

exercises.prompt: Malagu ya ing labuad ning bale mi.
accepted: the surroundings of our house are beautiful
the surroundings of our house are beautiful.
the area around our house is beautiful
the area around our house is beautiful.
our neighborhood is beautiful
our neighborhood is beautiful.
